blocks.text: SUBJECT: Bridge Deck Rehabilitation, Peck-Jennings. Project setup and workstreams.

The project file is open and lettered. The start date of record is {{fact:f:E-2007-001.start}}, and everything below runs from that date. This memo covers what we are producing, who has which part, and how the drawings are set up, so that nobody has to ask twice. It is a short schedule and it stays short only if the front end is done properly, which is what this is about.

{{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}} has retained us for the design and for bid-period assistance, nothing beyond that. What we owe is a sealed plan set and a specification for the rehabilitation of the deck and its approaches, complete enough that a contractor can bid it without a pile of questions and build it without a pile of change orders. There are two submittals, preliminary at sixty percent and final for bidding. Plan the work backward from the preliminary, not forward from today.

items: Field survey and condition documentation. Pamela Warren. Deck, joints, railing, approach pavement, and drainage, measured and photographed. Field books live in the project folder, not in a truck.

Base drawings, layering, and the plotting setup. Mine. The template is the firm's, unchanged. If a layer needs to exist that does not, ask me and I will add it and tell everybody. Do not invent one, and do not move one that is already there.
Standard details and approach grading sheets. Kimberly Branch. Details come out of the detail notebook first. We draw a new one only when there is nothing that fits.
Specifications. Pamela Warren, with the Principal Engineer's review before anything is typed clean.
Cost estimate. Pamela Warren, at the preliminary submittal and again at final.
Correspondence and the project file. Everyone. Anything sent or received gets filed the day it moves, and a fax gets its transmittal sheet stapled to it.

blocks.text: Two things worth stating now rather than after they have gone wrong. First, the sheet numbering is fixed at the index and does not get renumbered partway through, because the specification and the estimate will both refer to sheets by number and I am not chasing those references twice. If a sheet has to be added late it gets a letter suffix and lives where it belongs. Second, nothing is plotted for issue from a personal directory. The set plots from the project folder, on the firm's pens, or it does not go out.
The cover sheet is drawn last and it is drawn once. It carries the seal, and it is not a place to experiment with the title block.

Carol Lewis, Director of Operations at Peck-Jennings, is the client contact. Anything in writing that goes to her leaves this office over the Principal Engineer's signature, not ours. Telephone questions about site access go to her directly, and they get written in the project file the same day, whatever the answer was. If she asks for something that looks like a change in scope, do not agree to it on the telephone. Write down what she asked for and bring it back here.

items: I will open the drawing set and issue the sheet index this week. Nobody starts a sheet before the index exists.
Pamela Warren will telephone to arrange the first site visit and to ask for the record drawings. If the record drawings do not exist, we find that out now rather than at sixty percent.
Kimberly Branch will pull the details we expect to use and mark the ones that need editing.
We will meet in the conference room at the end of next week. Bring what you have, not what you intend to have.

blocks.text: Nothing about this job is unusual. The one thing that will hurt us is a survey that comes back thin and gets discovered late, so it is worth an extra half day in the field now.
